findings.description: A negative max_length makes slug[:max_length] slice from the end, silently dropping trailing characters instead of truncating, with no guard against negative values.
Fully masking short values still emits one mask char per input char, leaking the exact length of the secret.
The non-short branch preserves input length exactly, leaking the secret's length to an observer.
splitlines() plus '\n'.join drops any trailing newline and normalizes unusual line separators (\v, \f, \x85, \u2028), altering the text's structure.
A multi-character mask_char breaks the implied length invariant since mask_char * len(value) yields a string longer than the input.

A multi-character suffix can let the result exceed limit when len(suffix) < limit but suffix occupies more than the reserved tail space is not the case here, yet truncation counts code points so grapheme clusters/astral chars may be split.
No guard against None or non-str input; callers passing None raise an opaque TypeError despite the str type hint.
DP allocates an O(len(b)) row without swapping so the shorter string is iterated; passing a long b uses more memory than O(min(len(a),len(b))).
A negative spaces value yields an empty pad and silently performs no indentation instead of erroring.
When len(suffix) >= limit the function returns a truncated suffix that may itself be a partial grapheme, producing a visually broken marker.
